data_in.description: GNPS public mass spectral library containing spectra with salt SMILES annotations

data_out.description: Cleaned spectrum library with repaired SMILES for salt-containing spectra, where multi-component salt SMILES have been replaced with matching ion SMILES validated by parent mass

domain_knowledge: Multi-component salt SMILES encoding represents both cation and anion as separate molecular entities within a single SMILES string, whereas the measured fragment in MS corresponds to a single ion after dissociation.

Parent mass (monoisotopic mass) is calculated from the molecular formula of the intact neutral molecule, but the precursor m/z reflects the actual ion detected, requiring adjustment by the adduct mass difference for comparison.

Ion fragmentation patterns in MS/MS are specific to the actual ion being measured; incorrect salt SMILES annotations can lead to failed spectral library matching because the theoretical fragments will not correspond to observed fragments.

RDKit's molecular weight calculation must use monoisotopic masses (exact masses of individual isotopes), not average atomic weights, to match the precursor m/z precision of high-resolution mass spectrometry data.

direct_checks: verify file exists at github.com/matchms/matchms containing the 'repair_smiles_of_salts' filter implementation
verify file exists at 10.5281/zenodo.11657300 containing matchms source code with salt SMILES repair filter
script_runs: execute the repair_smiles_of_salts filter on a test dataset of spectra with multi-component salt SMILES annotations
output_matches_reference: verify that output spectra contain SMILES field with single-component ion SMILES (not multi-component salts) when parent mass validation succeeds
value_in_range: verify that parent mass of corrected spectrum matches the monoisotopic mass of the output ion SMILES (within tolerance)
verify file_format_is for output spectra: MGF, JSON, or other structured format consistent with matchms spectrum object serialization
row_count_equals or contains_substring: verify that spectra originally annotated with multi-component salt SMILES are present in output with repaired single-component SMILES
robust to parameter choices: verify that the filter correctly handles salts with varying ion charge states and different salt component combinations, no canonical answer
expert_review: Verify that the parent-mass matching logic correctly identifies which ion (cation or anion) from a salt dissociation should replace the full salt SMILES, based on observed precursor m/z and ionization mode
Confirm that the ion SMILES selected by the filter is chemically appropriate for the salt compound (e.g., correct protonation state, charge, isotope)
Assess whether all major salt dissociation patterns (e.g., Na+, K+, Cl−, acetate, etc.) are handled by the filter or whether important salt types are missing
Review whether the filter correctly preserves other metadata fields (compound name, InChI, InChIKey) when replacing the salt SMILES, or regenerates them consistently

inputs: GNPS public mass spectral library containing spectra with salt SMILES annotations
methodology_summary: Load spectrum library and filter spectra with salt SMILES annotations
Decompose salt SMILES into constituent ions and calculate monoisotopic masses
Match ion masses against spectrum parent mass with tolerance window
Replace salt SMILES with validated ion SMILES when parent mass match is found
Output corrected spectrum metadata to cleaned library file
missing_information: The discussion states that the pipeline currently lacks plausibility checks that consider both metadata and measured fragments, and explicitly notes future expansions might include filters checking if fragments match the given annotation, but the article does not specify whether the 'repair SMILES of salts' filter uses fragment ion matching or relies only on parent mass and precursor m/z.

The article does not provide details on the tolerance threshold or algorithmic specifics used by the repair_smiles_of_salts filter to match parent mass against salt ion combinations, or how ambiguous cases (multiple ions within tolerance) are resolved.

The article does not explicitly report error rates, validation accuracy, or sensitivity/specificity metrics specific to the 'repair SMILES of salts' filter alone (only aggregate metrics across all repair functions are given: 52,084 repaired out of 83,843 candidate spectra).

task_description: Apply the 'repair SMILES of salts' filter to spectra with multi-component salt annotations, replacing full salt SMILES with the matching ion SMILES when parent-mass validation succeeds. Output the corrected spectrum metadata.

task_inputs.label: GNPS public mass spectral library with salt annotations

task_objective: Apply the 'repair SMILES of salts' filter to salt-encoded spectra and output corrected SMILES strings where parent mass validates the ion component match.

task_outputs.label: Spectra with corrected salt SMILES annotations

title: Filter: repair SMILES of salts by matching parent mass against individual or combined salt ion masses
uncertainty_notes: This card was generated by the LLM-assisted pipeline and needs scientific expert review.
Each TracedClaim's evidence_span has been substring-checked against its source section; see logs/llm_calls.jsonl and capsules/<task_id>/quality_report.json for groundedness results.
workflow_description: 1. Load spectra from GNPS library with salt-encoded SMILES annotations using matchms. 2. Identify spectra whose SMILES encode multi-component salts (e.g., sodium chloride, ammonium acetate) by parsing SMILES structure with RDKit. 3. For each salt SMILES, generate all possible ion combinations (cation and anion fragments) and calculate their monoisotopic masses. 4. Compare each ion mass against the spectrum's parent mass (precursor m/z adjusted for adduct) to find matches within tolerance. 5. When a match is found, replace the full salt SMILES with the matching ion SMILES in the spectrum's molecular annotation. 6. Output repaired spectra with corrected SMILES metadata to cleaned library file.
